name: All-or-Nothing Thinking
definition: Seeing things in only two categories rather than on a continuum. If a situation falls short of perfect, you see it as a total failure.
example: After getting a B+ on a test: "I didn't get an A, so I completely failed."
soundsLike: I always... / I never... / It's either perfect or worthless / Complete success or total failure
realityCheck: Most things exist on a spectrum. A B+ is a strong grade. Partial success is still success.

name: Overgeneralization
definition: Drawing broad negative conclusions from a single event. One bad experience becomes a never-ending pattern of defeat.
example: After one awkward conversation: "I always say the wrong thing. No one ever wants to talk to me."
soundsLike: This always happens / Everyone thinks... / Nothing ever works out / I'll never be able to...
realityCheck: One event doesn't define a pattern. Look for counter-examples — times when things went differently.

name: Mental Filter
definition: Focusing exclusively on the negative detail in a situation while filtering out all the positive aspects.
example: Getting praised by 9 colleagues but fixating on the one person who gave critical feedback: "Everyone thinks my work is bad."
soundsLike: But what about that one thing that went wrong... / The only thing that matters is...
realityCheck: Zoom out. What are you filtering out? If you listed everything that happened, what would the full picture look like?

name: Disqualifying the Positive
definition: Dismissing positive experiences by insisting they "don't count" for some reason. Maintaining a negative belief despite contradicting evidence.
example: After a successful presentation: "They were just being nice. It wasn't really that good."
soundsLike: That doesn't count because... / They only said that to be polite / Anyone could have done that / It was just luck
realityCheck: Would you dismiss a friend's achievement this way? Positive evidence is still evidence.

name: Mind Reading
definition: Assuming you know what other people are thinking without evidence. Usually assuming they are thinking negatively about you.
example: A coworker walks past without saying hello: "She's angry at me. She must think I did something wrong."
soundsLike: They think I'm... / I know they're judging me / She probably hates me / He must be disappointed
realityCheck: You can't read minds. There are many possible explanations. What evidence do you actually have?

name: Fortune Telling
definition: Predicting that things will turn out badly without evidence. Treating a negative prediction as an established fact.
example: Before a job interview: "I'm going to freeze up and embarrass myself. There's no point in going."
soundsLike: I just know it will go badly / There's no point because... / It's going to be a disaster / This will never work
realityCheck: Predictions aren't facts. How many times have your worst predictions actually come true? What's the most likely outcome?

name: Catastrophizing
definition: Blowing things out of proportion. Exaggerating the importance of a negative event or your shortcomings.
example: Making a small mistake at work: "This is a disaster. I'm going to get fired. My career is over."
soundsLike: This is the worst thing that could happen / I can't handle this / Everything is ruined / This is catastrophic
realityCheck: What is the actual, realistic worst case? And how likely is it? Will this matter in a week? A month? A year?

name: Minimization
definition: Shrinking the importance of positive events, qualities, or achievements. The flip side of catastrophizing.
example: After finishing a marathon: "It wasn't that big of a deal. Lots of people do it."
soundsLike: It's nothing / Anyone could do that / It doesn't really matter / I got lucky
realityCheck: If a friend did this, would you minimize it? Your achievements deserve recognition.

name: Emotional Reasoning
definition: Assuming that your negative emotions reflect the way things really are. "I feel it, therefore it must be true."
example: "I feel like a failure, so I must be a failure." or "I feel anxious, so something bad must be about to happen."
soundsLike: I feel [emotion], so it must be true / I just feel like... / My gut tells me...
realityCheck: Feelings are information, not facts. Anxiety doesn't mean danger. Feeling stupid doesn't mean you are.

name: Should Statements
definition: Using rigid rules about how you or others should behave. Leads to guilt when directed at yourself and frustration when directed at others.
example: "I should be further along in my career by now." or "They should have known that would upset me."
soundsLike: I should... / I must... / I ought to... / They should have... / I have to...
realityCheck: Replace 'should' with 'I'd prefer' or 'It would be nice if.' Who made this rule? Is it realistic?

name: Labeling
definition: Attaching a fixed, global label to yourself or others based on a single event. Instead of describing the behavior, you define the person.
example: After forgetting a friend's birthday: "I'm a terrible person" instead of "I forgot my friend's birthday."
soundsLike: I'm a loser / He's an idiot / She's so selfish / I'm worthless
realityCheck: You are not your worst moment. Describe the behavior, not the person. One action doesn't define who you are.

name: Personalization
definition: Taking excessive responsibility for events that are not entirely under your control. Blaming yourself for something that isn't entirely your fault.
example: Your child gets a bad grade: "This is my fault. I'm a bad parent."
soundsLike: It's all my fault / If only I had... / They're upset because of me
realityCheck: Many factors contribute to any outcome. What percentage is realistically yours? What factors were outside your control?

name: Blaming
definition: Holding other people entirely responsible for your emotional pain or problems. The opposite of personalization.
example: "My boss makes me feel anxious. It's completely their fault I'm stressed."
soundsLike: It's all their fault / They made me feel... / If they hadn't... / I can't help it because of them
realityCheck: Others influence your feelings, but you have some agency in how you respond. What's in your control here?

name: Fallacy of Fairness
definition: Believing that life should always be fair, and feeling resentful when it isn't. Measuring every situation by what you think is fair.
example: "I worked harder than everyone else — it's not fair that I didn't get the promotion."
soundsLike: It's not fair / I deserve better / They got what they didn't deserve / Life should be...
realityCheck: Fairness is subjective. Life doesn't always follow our rules. Focus on what you can control rather than what should be.
